topics.title: Spring Boot Test Slices
topics.description: Spring Boot test slices load only a specific slice of the ApplicationContext — just the web layer, just the JPA layer, etc. This makes tests faster and more focused than @SpringBootTest (which loads everything). Key slices: @WebMvcTest (controllers + security, no service/repo beans), @DataJpaTest (JPA + H2/real DB, no web beans), @JsonTest (Jackson serialization only), @RestClientTest (RestTemplate mocking).
topics.story: A team ran @SpringBootTest for every test. Each test took 8 seconds to start because it loaded the full context — Kafka, Redis, Email service, AWS clients, all of it — even for a test that only checked whether a JSON field was null. After switching to test slices: @WebMvcTest tests start in 0.8 seconds. @DataJpaTest tests start in 1.2 seconds. Total test suite dropped from 4 minutes to 35 seconds. The key insight: load only what you need. A controller test doesn't need a real Kafka producer.
topics.analogy: @SpringBootTest = load the entire application (slow, realistic). @WebMvcTest = load only controllers, filters, security config, ControllerAdvice (fast, web-only). @DataJpaTest = load only JPA repositories, entity scanning, datasource (fast, DB-only). @MockBean = add a Spring-managed mock into the partial context. MockMvc = simulate HTTP requests without a real server. @Autowired MockMvc in @WebMvcTest — automatically configured. perform(get('/api/users')) — fire a request. andExpect(status().isOk()) — assert response. andExpect(jsonPath('$.name').value('Alice')) — assert JSON.
topics.memoryAnchor: @WebMvcTest(UserController.class) — loads only UserController and web infrastructure. @MockBean UserService service — mock the service layer. @Autowired MockMvc mvc — HTTP simulator. mvc.perform(get('/api/users/1')).andExpect(status().isOk()). @DataJpaTest — loads JPA, H2 by default, no web beans. @AutoConfigureTestDatabase(replace=NONE) — use real DB instead of H2. @Rollback(false) — keep data after test (for debugging). given/when/then with MockitoAnnotations. @JsonTest — only loads Jackson configuration. @RestClientTest — loads RestTemplate + MockRestServiceServer.
topics.firstPrinciples: @SpringBootTest scans and loads the entire ApplicationContext. Each additional bean adds startup time and potential side effects. Test slices work by excluding most auto-configurations: @WebMvcTest excludes @Repository, @Service, @Component by default — only @Controller, @ControllerAdvice, @JsonComponent, WebMvcConfigurer, Filter beans are loaded. Services are provided as @MockBean. @DataJpaTest excludes @Service, @Controller, @Component — only @Repository beans, entity scanning, datasource, and transaction management are loaded. This focused context starts 5-10x faster.
topics.bruteForce: @SpringBootTest for every test: loads Kafka consumers, Redis clients, email services, AWS SDK, database connection pool — even when testing a simple controller method. Startup: 8-12 seconds per test class. 50 test classes = 400-600 seconds just for context startup.
topics.optimizedApproach: @WebMvcTest for controller logic + security. @DataJpaTest for repository queries. @SpringBootTest only for true end-to-end integration tests (2-3 per service). Average context startup: 0.5-2 seconds per slice. Same 50 test classes: 25-100 seconds total.
topics.whenToUse: @WebMvcTest: controller request mapping, validation, security rules, JSON serialization, error handling. @DataJpaTest: custom JPQL queries, named queries, pagination, findBy* method correctness. @JsonTest: DTO serialization/deserialization correctness. @SpringBootTest: full stack smoke tests, end-to-end flows. NOT: @WebMvcTest for testing service logic (use plain JUnit + Mockito instead).

examples.title: WebMvcTest + DataJpaTest Examples
examples.explanation: @WebMvcTest loads only the controller and Spring MVC infrastructure. @MockBean creates a Spring-managed mock for the service — given/verify comes from Mockito. MockMvc.perform() simulates HTTP without starting a real server — no port, no network. @DataJpaTest uses H2 by default (replace = true) but @AutoConfigureTestDatabase(replace=NONE) switches to the configured datasource. TestEntityManager is a test-specific helper for JPA operations. @JsonTest only loads Jackson — fastest possible test for serialization correctness.
examples.realWorldUse: Project structure: 30 @WebMvcTest tests (controller validation, security rules) + 20 @DataJpaTest tests (custom query correctness) + 5 @SpringBootTest tests (full-stack smoke). Total CI time: ~45 seconds. Without slices with @SpringBootTest everywhere: same 55 tests × 8 seconds startup = 7+ minutes just for context startup.

problems.title: HTTP Status Code Mapping
problems.description:
Given N REST operations and their expected outcomes, determine the correct HTTP status code.

Input per line: `METHOD /path SCENARIO`

Scenarios and expected codes:
- `GET /resource FOUND` → 200
- `GET /resource NOT_FOUND` → 404
- `POST /resource CREATED` → 201
- `POST /resource INVALID` → 400
- `PUT /resource UPDATED` → 200
- `DELETE /resource DELETED` → 204
- `GET /resource UNAUTHORIZED` → 401
- `GET /resource FORBIDDEN` → 403

Sample input:
```
4
GET /users/1 FOUND
GET /users/99 NOT_FOUND
POST /users CREATED
POST /users INVALID
```
Expected:
```
200
404
201
400
```
problems.hint1: Build a Map<String, Integer> of SCENARIO → status code.
problems.hint2: Extract the scenario (last token) from each input line.
problems.hint3: Look up the status code and print it.
problems.sampleInput:
4
GET /users/1 FOUND
GET /users/99 NOT_FOUND
POST /users CREATED
POST /users INVALID
problems.editorial: In @WebMvcTest, andExpect(status().isOk()) verifies 200, andExpect(status().isNotFound()) verifies 404, andExpect(status().isCreated()) verifies 201. Knowing the expected HTTP status for each scenario is fundamental for writing correct controller tests. @ControllerAdvice maps exceptions to status codes — @ResponseStatus(HttpStatus.NOT_FOUND) or ResponseEntityExceptionHandler.

problems.title: JsonPath Query Evaluator
problems.description:
Simulate JsonPath evaluation on simple JSON objects. Given a JSON object (simple key:value pairs, no nesting) and JsonPath queries of form `$.key`, return the value or `NOT_FOUND`.

Input:
- First line: N key-value pairs as `key=value` separated by spaces
- Second line: M queries
- Next M lines: `$.key`

Sample input:
```
name=Alice age=25 role=ADMIN
3
$.name
$.age
$.email
```
Expected:
```
Alice
25
NOT_FOUND
```
problems.hint1: Parse key=value pairs into a Map.
problems.hint2: For each query `$.key`, extract the key by removing `$.`.
problems.hint3: Look up in the Map. If not found, print NOT_FOUND.
problems.editorial: In @WebMvcTest tests, jsonPath('$.name').value('Alice') uses the JsonPath library to navigate JSON responses. $.key accesses top-level fields. $.nested.key accesses nested objects. $.array[0].key accesses array elements. NOT_FOUND in this context corresponds to jsonPath('$.email').doesNotExist(). Understanding JsonPath syntax is essential for writing readable, precise MockMvc assertions.

problems.title: Test Slice Selection
problems.description:
Given N test class descriptions, output the optimal Spring Boot test annotation: `@WebMvcTest`, `@DataJpaTest`, `@JsonTest`, or `@SpringBootTest`.

Rules:
- Only tests REST endpoints, HTTP status, JSON response → @WebMvcTest
- Only tests database queries, JPA repository methods → @DataJpaTest  
- Only tests JSON serialization/deserialization → @JsonTest
- Tests full request-to-database flow → @SpringBootTest

Sample:
```
4
tests controller returns 404 when user not found
tests findByEmail JPQL query returns correct result
tests DTO serializes createdAt as ISO date string
tests POST /orders creates order and publishes Kafka event
```
Expected:
```
@WebMvcTest
@DataJpaTest
@JsonTest
@SpringBootTest
```
problems.hint1: Keywords for @WebMvcTest: controller, HTTP status, REST endpoint, 404/200/401, MockMvc.
problems.hint2: Keywords for @DataJpaTest: JPQL, query, repository, findBy, database, JPA.
problems.hint3: Keywords for @JsonTest: serializ, deserializ, JSON, DTO, Jackson. @SpringBootTest: end-to-end, full flow, kafka, redis.
problems.sampleInput:
4
tests controller returns 404 when user not found
tests findByEmail JPQL query returns correct result
tests DTO serializes createdAt as ISO date string
tests POST /orders creates order and publishes Kafka event
problems.editorial: Choosing the right test slice is a key skill. @WebMvcTest for web layer — loads controllers but mocks services. @DataJpaTest for persistence layer — loads JPA context with in-memory DB by default. @JsonTest for Jackson configuration — fastest, loads nothing but Jackson. @SpringBootTest for true integration — loads full context. Rule of thumb: use the most focused slice that tests what you care about.
